    		 3.0/10 RESISTANT

    Click any sector name for full details. Hover scores for explanation.
    Scores: 10 = strongest/most favorable, 1 = weakest/least favorable. For Legal Friction, 10 = lowest friction (most favorable). Composite = average across all 7 dimensions on the 1-10 scale.

    NASSCOM AI Adoption Index 2.0 IBEF Manufacturing WEF Future of Jobs 2025 McKinsey India Manufacturing
    ▶ Show Scoring Rationale — Why Auto & FMCG Won

    🚗 Auto: 7.9/10 — Won on Categories 1, 4 & 7

    Cat 1: TAM Size (10/10) — Largest enterprise TAM at $1,235M. 148K Auto Core brand-owner on-payroll Knowledge Workers in the 11 selected Auto workflow nodes (subset of 219K universe) + ~450K ecosystem (dealer network $201M + ancillary suppliers $180M). Salary $30,679/yr from 30 listed Auto compensation survey. Physical and plant-OT workflows fall outside the knowledge-worker scope and are not counted.

    Cat 4: AI & Digital Readiness (10/10) — Highest NASSCOM AI score. OEMs like M&M and Tata Motors already deploying AI. IATF 16949 creates structured data.

    Cat 7: Overall Readiness (10/10) — EV transition doubles engineering complexity, PLI-Auto ($3.5B), 12,250 SME supplier base creates massive distribution channel.

    Weakness: Cat 2 (Workforce Friction) at 6/10 — lower attrition than FMCG means less immediate hiring pain.

    🛒 FMCG: 8.7/10 — Won on Categories 2, 3 & 6

    Cat 2: Workforce Friction (10/10) — 40-60% field force attrition = continuous recruitment treadmill. Strongest pull for AI.

    Cat 3: Outsourcing (10/10) — Already delegates extensively to BPOs. Lowest resistance to AI delegation.

    Cat 6: Financial Capacity (10/10) — 17% net margin = highest of any manufacturing sector. Budget headroom for technology.

    Cat 1: TAM Size (9/10) — $595M enterprise TAM, second-largest of the six sectors. 105K FMCG Core brand-owner on-payroll Knowledge Workers in the 17 selected FMCG workflow nodes at $14,469/yr avg salary (from 13 listed FMCG compensation survey; 2026) + ~500K distributor / merchandiser channel ecosystem. Highest Knowledge Worker/revenue density (1,300/$B); strong Sales + Marketing concentration drives higher addressability than Auto.

    Weakness: Cat 4 (AI Readiness) at 6/10 — digital maturity lower than Auto/ESDM, but Quick Commerce (70-80% CAGR) is forcing rapid digitization.

    04 — AI Automation TAM

    Having selected Auto (7.9/10) and FMCG (8.7/10) from the 7-dimension sector-scoring matrix, Section 04 sizes the addressable market in both. Method: enumerate firms, count the Knowledge Workers inside AI-addressable workflows, price the automatable share of their tasks, discount below labour cost. Three Serviceable Markets (Core / Extended / Ecosystem) sum to the $1.8B TAM.

    Knowledge Work Automation TAM
    $1.8B TAM · ~1,150 firms · ~1.2M workers — full breakdownClick to expand
    Knowledge Work Automation TAM
    $1.8B
    • Core SAM — $1,065M: enterprise brand-owners (≥₹1,000 Cr revenue), closable today.
    • Extended SAM — $105M: mid-market brand-owners (≥₹100 Cr + ≥200 KW), reachable 2–4 yrs.
    • Ecosystem SAM — $659M: channel workforce (dealers, ancillary, distributors, merchandisers).
    • By sector: Auto $1,235M · FMCG $595M.
    Target Brand-owner Companies
    ~1,150
    • What is a brand-owner: Indian firm that owns the consumer-facing brand (Maruti, HUL). Not dealers, suppliers, or distributors.
    • Core (295 firms): enterprise-grade, closable today via direct sales (138 Auto + 157 FMCG, ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue, after removing 43 commodity / agri-input firms from the master list).
    • Extended (825 firms): mid-market, reachable via PLG (425 Auto + 400 FMCG, ₹100–999 Cr; ~135 mid-market firms named individually; the rest sized by industry-association data via ACMA + CRISIL + Brickwork + Kantar + Inc42).
    • Ecosystem (30 named firms): distributor + merchandiser channel for FMCG; Auto channel (FADA dealers + ACMA ancillaries) sized aggregate not enumerated.
    • By sector: Auto 563 (138 Core + 425 Extended) · FMCG 587 (157 Core + 400 Extended + 30 Ecosystem). BRSR-anchor strict subset for direct KW parsing: 252 firms (139 Auto + 113 FMCG, ≥₹1,000 Cr AND ≥1,000 KW).
    Addressable Workers
    ~1.2M
    • Brand-owner KW (253K): 148K Auto + 105K FMCG inside the 28 selected workflow nodes.
    • Ecosystem KW (950K): 450K Auto channel + 500K FMCG channel.
    • Scope: brand-owner + channel workforce doing AI-automatable Knowledge Work.

    TAM derivation — 3 SAMs (Core + Extended + Ecosystem) summed to TAM

    The $1.8B Total Addressable Market splits into 3 Serviceable Addressable Markets (SAMs). Each SAM runs its own 7-step chain with Auto + FMCG shown side-by-side; every row shows the derivation method and primary sources. Per-node values sum EXACTLY to sector totals by construction (no scaling factors).

    TAM = Core SAM + Extended SAM + Ecosystem SAM. Core SAM targets enterprise brand-owners closable today; Extended SAM targets mid-market firms reachable over 2-4 years as AI pricing compresses; Ecosystem SAM captures the channel workforce (dealers, ancillary, distributors, merchandisers) executing brand-owner workflows.

    CORE SAM
    🎯 Core SAM
    Horizon: today · direct enterprise sales · Filter: ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue (brand-owner filter)
    Step · Derivation · Sources
    🚗 Auto (138 Core firms)
    🛒 FMCG (157 Core firms)
    STEP 1 Brand-owner universe
    Firms meeting Core filter
    Count of Indian brand-owner firms meeting the Core filter (≥₹1,000 Cr revenue (brand-owner filter)). These firms are closable today via enterprise sales.
    Method: Cross-referenced three registries: SIAM/ACMA member lists + Nifty Auto constituents + Ministry of Corporate Affairs revenue filings (Auto); BSE FMCG index + Screener.in + company IR (FMCG). Every firm verified across the 295-firm top-tier list and the 135-firm enumerated mid-market list.
    Sources: SIAM member list · ACMA directory · Nifty Auto Index · BSE FMCG Index · Screener.in · 295-firm Core registry
    138 Core firms
    MarutiTata MotorsM&MBajaj AutoHeroTVS
    157 Core firms
    HULITCNestleBritanniaDaburMarico
    STEP 2 Brand-owner Knowledge Workers
    × KW per firm
    On-payroll white-collar headcount across the enumerated Core firms. Each firm contributes its BRSR-parsed KW (45 direct filings) or stratified-density estimate (per tier-class: OEM / Tier-1 MNC / Tier-1 local / Tier-2 ancillary / tyre / dealer / FMCG-listed / FMCG-MNC / bottler / co-op). No single sector-wide density — tier-specific calibration from ~45 BRSR anchors.
    Method: Python parser extracts the "Permanent Employees" column from each company's sustainability filing PDF for 45 listed firms. For unlisted firms, revenue × firm-type density gives the estimate. Sum across all 295 firms = Core Knowledge Worker universe.
    Sources: SEBI BRSR Circular · BSE BRSR filings · HUL IR · Nestle India IR
    219K
    136K
    STEP 3 Selected-node workflows
    × 67.5% Auto / 76.9% FMCG
    Share of brand-owner KW sitting inside the 28 selected workflow nodes (11 Auto + 17 FMCG) picked for deep-dive AI relevance. Bottom-up, no scaling factors.
    Method: For each of 372 roles in the Role Library, classified to one of 57 workflow nodes; aggregated against 2026 compensation survey headcount distribution (49,434 Auto + 6,007 FMCG sampled). Share = Σ(role HC × role-in-selected-node flag) / Σ(role HC).
    Sources: Role Library (372 roles) · 2026 Comp Survey (49K Auto + 6K FMCG)
    147,770 (67.5%)
    104,811 (76.9%)
    STEP 4 Avg annual salary
    × $30,679 Auto / $14,469 FMCG
    Department-headcount-weighted average annual compensation across the selected-node KW pool. Engineering-heavy nodes pull Auto higher; admin-heavy nodes pull FMCG lower.
    Method: 2026 compensation survey triangulated against BRSR Principle 5 pay-band disclosures (P3 Professional / P4 Middle Mgmt / P5 Senior Mgmt) from the same 45 listed filings. Weighted by role HC mix.
    Sources: 2026 Comp Survey (49K Auto + 6K FMCG) · BSE BRSR filings
    $30,679
    $14,469
    STEP 5 Role eligibility
    × 85% Auto / 88% FMCG (per-node 72-98%)
    Share of selected-node KW in roles where ≥25% of tasks are AI-addressable (vs. pure physical/relationship work or execution roles outside AI scope).
    Method: Role-library cross-walk: each role scored against published AI task-exposure taxonomies (Goldman 2023, McKinsey 2024). Eligibility = HC-weighted share of AI-addressable roles in each sector.
    Sources: Role Library (372 roles) · Goldman Sachs AI Report · McKinsey State of AI 2024
    85%
    88%
    STEP 6 Task AI automation rate
    × 40% Auto / 38% FMCG (per-node 27-46%)
    Within eligible roles, share of the role’s day-to-day task minutes that current generative AI can automate (NOT fully replace — augment + eliminate combined).
    Method: Role-weighted from McKinsey “State of AI 2024” task-by-task automation potential + Goldman Sachs 2023 task-exposure index, cross-checked against a16z enterprise-AI catalog (product category maturity).
    Sources: McKinsey State of AI 2024 · Goldman Sachs AI Report · a16z
    40%
    38%
    STEP 7 AI price discount
    × 50%
    Convention: enterprise AI priced below the labour cost it displaces (typical 40–60% range) to drive adoption. Using 50% midpoint.
    Method: Adoption pricing convention from enterprise SaaS market research (a16z, Gartner, McKinsey); matches observed pricing spreads for AI copilots relative to loaded-cost-per-hour of displaced labour.
    Sources: a16z · McKinsey State of AI 2024
    50%
    50%
    = Core SAM
    $807M
    $259M
    Total: $1,065M
    EXTENDED SAM
    🌱 Extended SAM
    Horizon: 2-4 years · PLG / self-serve pricing · Filter: ≥₹100 Cr AND ≥200 Knowledge Workers (not in Core)
    Step · Derivation · Sources
    🚗 Auto (425 firms)
    🛒 FMCG (400 firms)
    STEP 1 Brand-owner universe
    Firms meeting Extended filter
    Mid-market brand-owner firms meeting the Extended filter (≥₹100 Cr revenue AND ≥200 Knowledge Workers) — not already in Core. Reachable over 2-4 years as AI pricing compresses via product-led growth / self-serve.
    Method: Same cross-referenced registries as Core, with revenue + KW floors lowered to 100 Cr / 200 KW; explicit exclusion of the 295 Core firms to avoid double-count.
    Sources: SIAM member list · ACMA directory · BSE FMCG Index · Screener.in · MCA21 · 295-firm Core registry
    425 firms
    Atul AutoIgarashi MotorsSetco AutoShivam AutotechUCALAmpere Electric
    400 firms
    Paper BoatBombay ShavingiD FreshPlummCaffeineForest Essentials
    STEP 2 Brand-owner Knowledge Workers
    × KW per firm
    Count of white-collar employees across the Extended tier mid-market firms. Most sit below the BRSR filing threshold so direct headcount is rare — firm count is industry-anchored (425 Auto + 400 FMCG = 825 firms, triangulated from ACMA, CRISIL, Brickwork Ratings, Kantar and Inc42 D2C tracker). Each firm gets a density-based headcount estimate (revenue × tier-appropriate density: Auto Tier-2 ~180, D2C FMCG ~350 KW per ₹1,000 Cr). Total Extended KW = 11K Auto + 30K FMCG selected-node workers.
    Method: Where BRSR filings exist (upper half of Extended), direct parsing; otherwise density-ratio extrapolation anchored against sector revenue left after subtracting Core revenue.
    Sources: BSE BRSR filings · MCA21
    ~84K
    ~65K
    STEP 3 Selected-node workflows
    × 67.5% Auto / 76.9% FMCG
    Share of mid-market brand-owner KW sitting inside the 28 selected workflow nodes (11 Auto + 17 FMCG). Bottom-up from the role library. Note: mid-market firms typically run leaner back-office vs. large-cap (more generalist roles, fewer pure specialists) — the large-cap role distribution is used as the best available proxy since mid-market firms are underrepresented in listed-company BRSR filings. Sensitivity: a ±5pp shift in selected-node share would move Extended SAM by ~$22M.
    Method: Role-library cross-walk with mid-market adjustment: 372 roles tagged to 57 nodes; composition compared against Screener.in + MCA21 footnotes of 400 Auto / 140 FMCG Extended firms where role-level disclosures exist.
    Sources: Role Library (372 roles) · Screener.in mid-market filings · MCA21 AOC-4
    ~7,500 (67.5%)
    ~23,200 (76.9%)
    STEP 4 Avg annual salary
    × $23,930 Auto / $12,299 FMCG
    Weighted average annual compensation for Knowledge Workers at ~400 Auto / ~140 FMCG mid-market brand-owners. Estimated from OEM-vs-ancillary entry-salary gap (~38% at entry narrowing to ~10–15% at senior management) and MNC-vs-domestic FMCG corporate-cost differential (14–16%) — blended across career streams gives ~22% compression for Auto ($30,679 → $23,930) and ~15% for FMCG ($14,469 → $12,299). FMCG compression is lower because most large-cap FMCG is MNC-owned (HUL, Nestle, P&G, Colgate) with tighter global pay bands, while Indian large-cap Auto (Tata, M&M, Maruti) already sits in domestic pay band structure.
    Method: Career-stream-weighted blended comp: Exec $180K/$145K · Sr Mgmt $72K/$54K · Mgmt $36K/$26K · Professional $24K/$17K · Para-Pro $14K/$10K (Auto/FMCG, 2026 bands). Applied 22% Auto compression (entry-gap 38% narrowed for senior convergence) and 15% FMCG compression (MNC-vs-domestic corporate cost proxy).
    Sources: Aon India Salary Survey (via IBEF) · FMCG compensation overview (Chhimwal) · ADB firm-size wage paper
    $23,930
    $12,299
    STEP 5 Role eligibility
    × 85% Auto / 88% FMCG (per-node 72-98%)
    Share of mid-market Knowledge Workers in roles where AI can address at least 25% of tasks. Each of the 372 roles in the Role Library carries its own eligibility score (Goldman Sachs + McKinsey MGI taxonomies). A Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain or HR team at a mid-market firm runs the same task taxonomies and software stacks as at a large-cap enterprise — what differs is adoption speed (captured in Step 6 automation rate) and change-management capacity (captured in Step 7 50% PLG discount), not eligibility itself. Sector average 85% Auto / 88% FMCG; per-node range 72–98%.
    Method: Each role is tagged AI-eligible or not based on AI applicability. Node-level eligibility = share of KW in AI-eligible roles for that node. Weighted avg across nodes = 85% Auto / 88% FMCG.
    Sources: Role Library (372 roles) · Goldman Sachs AI Report · McKinsey MGI
    85%
    88%
    STEP 6 Task AI automation rate
    × 25.8% Auto / 27.1% FMCG
    Share of eligible-role tasks addressable by current generative AI. Mid-market firms have materially older tech stacks, less ERP coverage, and fewer data engineers — which compresses the *achievable* AI rate even when theoretical task exposure is identical. Estimated compression from Core (40% → 25.8% Auto; 38% → 27.1% FMCG), blended from NASSCOM-EY AI Adoption Index (large-cap "Expert" vs SMB "Starter/Enthusiast" gap), EY India Cloud & Data Revolution (14pp cloud-maturity gap), and BCG India enterprise-vs-SMB AI adoption data. Upside case: as PLG-delivered AI products mature over the 2–4 year Extended horizon, the gap will close — current gap represents point-in-time capability, not long-run equilibrium.
    Method: Core achievable rate (40% / 38% node-weighted avg) from McKinsey MGI + Goldman role-HC task-exposure weighting (node-weighted avg Auto 40% / FMCG 38%). Extended mid-market compression flagged estimated from NASSCOM-EY AI Adoption Index 2.0 (0.8-point gap on 4-point maturity scale) and EY cloud-maturity delta (14pp infra gap), weighted to account for the portion of AI tasks accessible via model APIs without infra dependence.
    Sources: McKinsey State of AI 2024 · Goldman Sachs AI Report · NASSCOM-EY AI Adoption Index 2.0 · EY India Cloud & Data Revolution
    25.8%
    27.1%
    STEP 7 AI price discount
    × 50%
    Below-labour pricing convention for mid-market. Extended firms reach via PLG / self-serve pricing 2–4 years out, not enterprise sales cycles — so headline list price in this tier is already compressed vs. enterprise seats. Net effective discount vs. displaced labour cost remains 50% (at the midpoint of the observed 40–60% enterprise-AI pricing band). Notable: PLG pricing has proven stickier than enterprise contracts in mid-market because churn is lower when switching cost is zero.
    Method: Pricing benchmarked against observed mid-market AI SaaS price points (Notion AI, Canva AI, GitHub Copilot) ÷ loaded cost per hour of displaced Knowledge Work role. Anchored to a16z enterprise-AI catalog pricing survey.
    Sources: a16z Gen AI Apps catalog · McKinsey State of AI 2024 · NFX pricing benchmarks
    50%
    50%
    = Extended SAM
    $47M
    $58M
    Total: $105M
    ECOSYSTEM SAM
    🔗 Ecosystem SAM
    Horizon: parallel · same sales motion as brand-owner · Cohorts: Auto dealers + ancillary · FMCG distributors + merchandisers
    Step · Derivation · Sources
    🚗 Auto (~15K dealerships + ancillary)
    🛒 FMCG (~450K firms)
    STEP 1 Channel firm universe
    Cohorts outside brand-owner payroll
    Addressable channel partners doing Knowledge Work on behalf of brand-owners — dealerships + ancillary component suppliers (Auto); distributor firms + merchandiser agencies (FMCG).
    Method: Cohort census from industry associations: FADA authorized-dealer registry (Auto); ACMA members below BRSR filing threshold (Auto ancillary); AICPDF + FICCI channel surveys (FMCG distributor + merchandiser agencies).
    Sources: FADA · ACMA directory · AICPDF · FICCI
    ~15K dealerships (30K outlets) + ~800 ancillary per ACMA
    FADALandmark CarsPopular VehiclesACMABoschMotherson
    ~450K distributors + ~300 merch agencies
    AICPDFMetro Cash & CarryUdaanDMartFICCIBizom
    STEP 2 Channel workforce
    Headcount per cohort
    Total Knowledge Worker headcount across the channel. Dealer staff includes service advisors, sales, parts, admin; ancillary staff includes engineering + ops; distributors include sales + back-office; merchandiser agencies include field reps.
    Method: FADA: ~250K dealership KW staff (at ≈21 KW per authorised outlet); ACMA: ~200K ancillary KW staff below 30-co compensation survey cut; CII/FICCI: ~500K FMCG channel (distributor + merchandiser field reps).
    Sources: FADA · ACMA directory · CII · FICCI
    450K
    500K
    STEP 3 Selected-node workflows
    ≈ 100% (channel is in selected nodes by construction)
    Channel workforce is, by construction, in sales / service / distribution / field-ops workflows — all of which are selected nodes in the brand-owner value chain. So the selected-nodes filter keeps the full channel in scope.
    Method: Structural ratio: every role in the channel cohorts maps to a selected node. No carving needed.
    Sources: Role Library (372 roles)
    100%
    100%
    STEP 4 Avg annual compensation
    × $8.5K Auto / $6.0K FMCG
    Channel-cohort blended compensation. Lower than brand-owner because channel roles skew to field / variable-pay cohorts.
    Method: FADA dealer comp survey (≈$7K blended for service + sales) + ACMA ancillary survey (≈$10–12K for engineering-heavy ancillary) → $8.5K Auto channel blend. CII/FICCI FMCG channel survey → $6.0K FMCG channel blend.
    Sources: FADA · ACMA directory · CII · FICCI
    $8.5K
    $6.0K
    STEP 5 Role eligibility
    × 72% Auto / 58% FMCG
    Lower than brand-owner because channel roles include variable-pay field and commission cohorts that fall outside AI-addressable KW scope.
    Method: Cohort-weighted eligibility sized to the channel-anchored totals from FADA / ACMA / CII / FICCI surveys.
    Sources: FADA · FICCI
    72%
    58%
    STEP 6 Task AI automation rate
    × 27.7% Auto / 32% FMCG
    Cohort-weighted mix reflects field-sales + service-execution tasks vs. the brand-owner professional / engineering task mix.
    Method: Task AI rate weighted by the role mix of channel workforce (heavier in sales execution, lighter in analytical tasks) vs. Core professional/engineering mix.
    Sources: McKinsey State of AI 2024 · Goldman Sachs AI Report
    27.7%
    32%
    STEP 7 AI price discount
    × 50%
    50% below-labour pricing for channel-delivered AI. Ecosystem products route through DMS (Dealer Management Systems) and field-sales apps — where adoption pricing is even more aggressive than brand-owner SaaS, typically 40–55% of loaded labour cost. The 50% midpoint is used for consistency with brand-owner layers; channel price compression may push effective discount to 40% over time as Bizom / Field Assist / Reynolds & Reynolds India products mature. Dealer groups and distributor-tech platforms tend to standardize contracts quickly once a winner emerges, so pricing stability is higher here than in pure enterprise SaaS.
    Method: Pricing anchored to observed DMS + distributor-ERP SaaS list prices (Autoline, Reynolds India, Bizom, Field Assist) ÷ loaded cost per hour of displaced service-advisor / field-sales role.
    Sources: a16z Gen AI Apps catalog · Bizom India pricing · Field Assist
    50%
    50%
    = Ecosystem SAM
    $381M
    $278M
    Total: $659M
    TAM = Core SAM + Extended SAM + Ecosystem SAM
    🎯 Core SAM
    Auto $807M + FMCG $259M
    $1,065M
    🌱 Extended SAM
    Auto $47M + FMCG $58M
    $105M
    🔗 Ecosystem SAM
    Auto $381M + FMCG $278M
    $659M
    Total TAM
    3 SAMs summed · Auto + FMCG
    $1.8B

    Primary sources: ACMA FY24 presentation, Business Standard (ACMA confirmation), CRISIL FMCG, SEBI BRSR framework, 17 directly parsed BRSR PDFs (HUL, ITC, Nestle, Britannia, Dabur, Marico, Colgate, Tata Consumer, Maruti, Tata Motors, M&M, Bajaj, Hero, TVS, Eicher, Ashok Leyland, Bosch), WEF Future of Jobs 2025 and McKinsey MGI (per-role automation rates).

    Methodology FAQ — 9 questions mapped to the 7-step SAM chain + Ecosystem + Reconciliation

    Each FAQ row below is numbered and mapped to the step in the SAM tables above that it explains in detail. Click any row to expand the definition, per-sector math, and primary sources.

    Q1Step 1 — Brand-owner universeHow was the ~1,150-firm brand-owner universe (295 Core + 825 Extended + 30 Ecosystem) defined and split into Core (295 firms) + Extended (825 firms)? ▼
    Definition

    The brand-owner universe is the set of Indian companies whose employees the AI product is directly sold to — not their supply chain, not their customers, not their retail channel. Two filters split the universe into Core SAM (closable today via enterprise sales) and Extended SAM (reachable over 2–4 years via PLG / self-serve).

    Tier filterAuto firmsFMCG firmsRationale
    Core — ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue (brand-owner filter)138157Enterprise-grade firms with procurement + IT maturity to close today (after removing 43 commodity / agri-input firms)
    Extended — ≥₹100–999 Cr (industry-anchored)425400Mid-market, reachable as AI pricing compresses via PLG / self-serve. Industry-anchored via ACMA + CRISIL + Brickwork + Kantar + Inc42; ~135 mid-market firms named individually; the rest sized by industry-association data.
    Total brand-owner universe5635871,150 brand-owners (295 Core + 825 Extended industry-anchored + 30 named Ecosystem). Sector splits: Auto 563 (138 Core + 425 Extended); FMCG 587 (157 Core + 400 Extended + 30 Ecosystem).
    Primary registries cross-referenced
    • Auto: SIAM member list (OEMs) + ACMA directory (ancillary) + Nifty Auto (public OEMs) + MCA21 filings (long tail)
    • FMCG: BSE FMCG Index + Screener.in (public FMCG) + company IR pages (HUL, ITC, Nestle, Britannia, Dabur, Marico, Colgate, Tata Consumer)
    • Every Core firm is verified in the 295-firm top-tier list and the 135-firm enumerated mid-market list (preserved in the audit pack on GitHub) Thesis/company_universe/
    Q2Step 2 — Brand-owner Knowledge WorkersHow is "Knowledge Worker" defined and measured — BRSR-anchored definition ▼
    Definition

    Knowledge Workers in this thesis are permanent on-payroll employees classified as "Employees" (not "Workers") in the company's annual sustainability filing. This is the SEBI-mandated definition that splits a listed company's permanent workforce into (a) Employees — managerial, professional, technical, clerical, sales, HQ, R&D and (b) Workers — factory operators, machine tenders, direct production labour. The BRSR definition is used here because it is filed under SEBI compliance, primary-sourced for each company, and directly measurable.

    Why brand-owner on-payroll, not sector-wide

    The TAM denominator is permanent Knowledge Worker employees at non-MSME brand-owners only — not the sector total.

    Three exclusions:

    • MSME workforce — firms below ₹250 Cr turnover can’t afford enterprise AI SaaS.
    • Contract/gig workers — ~41% of factory labour sits outside the brand-owner’s payroll (ASI 2022–23), employed by staffing agencies.
    • Unorganised/informal sector — not addressable by enterprise software.

    Resulting universe: ~1.2M addressable Knowledge Workers — 253K brand-owner on-payroll (148K Auto + 105K FMCG in selected nodes) + ~950K ecosystem — sitting across 1,150 enumerated AI-addressable firms (295 Core at ≥₹1,000 Cr + 825 Extended at ₹100–999 Cr per ACMA / CRISIL / Kantar industry anchors).

    45 BRSR filings directly parsed (FY24 / FY24-25) — 17 primary anchor companies shown

    Every percentage below derives from the firm's annual workforce disclosure filed with the stock exchanges per SEBI BRSR circular. Links point to company investor-relations pages; filings accessible via BSE corporate filings search.

    FMCG (8): HUL 42.5% · ITC 50.7% · Nestle India 45.6% · Britannia 46.5% · Dabur 75.0% · Marico 96.6% · Colgate 37.1% · Tata Consumer 28.2%

    Auto (9): Maruti Suzuki 71.5% · Tata Motors 44.7% · M&M 76.9% · Bajaj Auto 47.1% · Hero MotoCorp 50.8% · TVS Motor 72.4% · Eicher 61.1% · Ashok Leyland 54.4% · Bosch India 55.5%

    Pooled Knowledge Worker share (sum of Knowledge Worker / sum of permanent): Auto 56.6% · FMCG 46.7%. Revenue-weighted: Auto 57.6% · FMCG 48.6%. Blended mean compensation from 2026 compensation survey (30 Auto + 13 FMCG listed cos, 55,441 WC employees): Auto ₹25.5L ($30.7K) · FMCG ₹12.0L ($14.5K). BRSR median pay (P5-E3) confirms the survey ranges.

    Q3Step 3 — Selected-node workflowsHow were the 28 selected workflow nodes picked from the 57-node value chain? ▼

    The 28 workflow nodes are selected from the complete 57-node value chain (28 Auto + 29 FMCG, of 57 total) for deep-dive AI addressable market sizing. Selection criteria: (a) white-collar knowledge-worker density > 40% of payroll, (b) at least one India-deployed AI product in category, (c) India-specific problem distinct from US/EU, (d) VC thesis coverage in 2024-2025. The remaining 29 nodes are either blue-collar dominant (plant-OT) or lower AI maturity.

    🚗 Auto — 11 nodes (of 28 total)
    1. Vehicle Program Leadership
    2. Testing & Validation
    3. APQP / PPAP Gate
    4. Supply Chain & Sourcing
    5. Supplier Quality & Planning
    6. Operations / Production Planning
    7. Dealership Management
    8. Showroom Sales
    9. After-Sales Service
    10. Warranty & Parts
    11. Finance
    12. Human Resources
    🛒 FMCG — 17 nodes (of 29 total)
    1. S&OP / Demand Planning
    2. Supply Planning
    3. Procurement
    4. Process Engineering
    5. Quality Control
    6. Food Safety / Regulatory
    7. Marketing
    8. Trade & Digital Mktg
    9. Sales Leadership
    10. Key Account Management
    11. Field Sales
    12. Retail Execution
    13. Customer Service
    14. Finance
    15. HR
    16. Regulatory & Compliance
    17. Analytics & Insights

    The remaining 29 non-selected nodes (R&D formulation, plant maintenance, logistics warehousing, etc.) are part of the knowledge-base but excluded from this TAM. They may move into scope in a future refresh as AI capability extends into plant-OT and formulation domains.

    Q4Step 4 — Avg annual salaryHow was the avg annual salary derived ($30,679 Auto / $14,469 FMCG)? ▼
    Definition

    Average annual compensation for one Knowledge Worker in the selected nodes. Computed as a role-HC-weighted average across the actual role mix of each sector, NOT a simple mean. Engineering-heavy Auto nodes pull the Auto average up; admin/sales/marketing-heavy FMCG nodes pull FMCG down.

    Primary data
    • 2026 compensation survey: 55,441 office employees across 43 listed companies.
      • 30 Automobile firms (49,434 employees): Maruti, Tata Motors, M&M, Bajaj, Hero, TVS, Eicher, Ashok Leyland, Bosch, Wabco, Sona, Sundram, Amara Raja, Wheels, Subros, Minda, Lumax, plus 13 more.
      • 13 FMCG firms (6,007 employees): HUL, ITC, Nestle, Britannia, Dabur, Marico, Colgate, P&G India, Reckitt, Tata Consumer, Adani Wilmar, Bikaji, Hatsun.
    • BRSR Principle 5 pay-band disclosures (P3 Professional / P4 Middle Mgmt / P5 Senior Mgmt) from the same 45 listed filings cross-verified the survey blended averages
    • Weighted by the role HC mix of each sector (engineering / finance / HR / sales / operations split from the 372-role Role Library)
    SectorSample size (employees)Avg annual compensationRole-mix driver
    🚗 Auto49,434$30,679Engineering R&D / program leadership / testing pull average up
    🛒 FMCG6,007$14,469Admin / sales / marketing mix keeps average lower

    Why not sector-wide averages: Sector averages from NITI Aayog / EPFO blend blue- and white-collar pay, understating the KW-specific figure. The the annual workforce-disclosure section (split) (Employees vs. Workers) isolates white-collar only.

    Q5Step 5 — Role eligibilityHow is "role eligibility" defined and why 85% Auto / 88% FMCG (per-node 72-98%)? ▼
    Definition

    Role eligibility is the share of Knowledge Worker payroll that can plausibly be automated by AI at the role level, before applying task-level automation rates.

    Two cohorts carved out (0% eligible):

    • Executives — CXOs and direct reports whose work is decision authority, judgement under accountability, and stakeholder management.
    • Hyper-technical R&D — principal scientists, lead architects, patent-holders. Tacit-knowledge work today’s commercial AI cannot displace.

    Everything else is eligible — clerical, sales, operations, mid-management, technical, standard professional. Because R&D payroll weight differs by sector, the eligibility filter lands at 85% Auto / 88% FMCG on average (per-node 72–98%).

    Per-sector cohort math (payroll-weighted)

    Why payroll-weighted, not headcount-weighted: excluded roles (CXO + hyper-technical R&D) earn disproportionately more per head, so payroll weighting captures their economic impact correctly.

    Cohort-weight sources: 2026 compensation survey of 30 Auto + 13 FMCG listed firms (55,441 employees) cross-checked against the median-pay disclosure in the compensation section of the sustainability report pay bands and McKinsey MGI role taxonomy.

    Why FMCG is lower: a heavier formulation and food-technology R&D cohort carries a higher payroll share of hyper-technical work, which is why its eligibility filter lands below Auto’s.

    🚗 Automobile — 85% avg (72-98% per-node)
    Executive / CXO / VP~5% payroll0%
    Hyper-technical R&D~13% payroll0%
    Professional (rest)~54% payroll100%
    Clerical + Sales + Mid-Mgmt + Tech + CS~28% payroll100%
    Eligible payroll85%
    🛒 FMCG — 88% avg (75-98% per-node)
    Executive / CXO / VP~6% payroll0%
    Hyper-technical R&D (food/formulation)~19% payroll0%
    Professional (rest)~50% payroll100%
    Clerical + Sales + Mid-Mgmt + Tech + CS~25% payroll100%
    Eligible payroll88%
    Where this shows up in the TAM calc

    Role eligibility is applied as Step 3 of the sector derivation. It is kept separate from Step 4 (Task AI rate) so each lever can be stress-tested independently. See the "Task AI rate" explainer below for Step 4.

    Sources: McKinsey MGI "Generative AI and the Future of Work", OECD AI-and-labour 2023, BRSR pay-band reconciliation for 17 companies directly parsed, and the 2026 compensation survey of 30 Auto + 13 FMCG listed brand-owners.

    Q6Step 6 — Task AI automation rateHow is task automation rate defined, and how do per-node rates combine into 40% Auto / 38% FMCG? ▼
    Definition

    Task AI rate is the share of tasks inside an eligible role that today’s commercial AI can credibly perform. It is applied after Role Eligibility (Step 3) and before the 2028 Automation Uplift (Step 5). Rates are payroll-weighted across the Role Library because high-paid cohorts do fewer routine tasks — a headcount-weighted average would overstate the addressable fraction.

    Per-role task automation rates (averaged from WEF + McKinsey MGI + Frey-Osborne)
    Clerical
    back-office, data entry
    77%
    Customer Service
    support & call-centre
    62%
    Technician
    QA, lab, field engineers
    47%
    Middle Mgmt
    planners, ops leads
    42%
    Sales (field)
    territory / account reps
    37%
    Professional
    engineers, analysts
    30%
    Senior Mgmt
    functional heads
    20%
    Executive / CXO
    ineligible (Step 3 filter)
    —
    Per-sector payroll-weighted aggregate
    🚗 Automobile — 40% task automation rate (avg)
    Role mix skews Professional (engineering) + Middle Mgmt (production planning). Professional at 30% and Middle Mgmt at 42% dominate — the payroll-weighted average across eligible Auto roles lands at 40%.
    🛒 FMCG — 38% task automation rate (avg)
    Role mix skews Sales/Field (37%) + Customer Service (62%) + Marketing/Analytics. Higher share of Clerical + Customer Service + Sales cohorts lifts the payroll-weighted average to 38%.

    Rates are today’s commercial-AI baseline. Rates are today's commercial-AI baseline; no forward-view automation uplift is applied — the task AI rate shown here is the single automation multiplier applied. Sources: WEF Future of Jobs 2025, McKinsey MGI GenAI 2023, Frey-Osborne 2013, OECD AI-and-labour 2023, averaged and applied via the 372-role Role Library.

    Per-role breakdown — combined sector rate
    Definition

    For each eligible role, the AI automation rate is the share of the role’s task portfolio today’s commercial AI tooling can credibly perform. These rates are task-time weighted (not salary-weighted) — each source codes role-level task inventories against current AI capability. Per-role rates come from three reconciled sources: WEF Future of Jobs 2025, McKinsey MGI GenAI 2023, and Frey-Osborne 2013 — averaged per career stream.

    Per-role task-automation rates (applied in our Role Library)
    Clerical
    back-office, data entry
    77% rate
    Customer Service
    support & call-centre
    62% rate
    Technician
    QA, lab, field engineers
    47% rate
    Middle Mgmt
    planners, ops leads
    42% rate
    Sales (field)
    territory / account reps
    37% rate
    Professional
    engineers, analysts
    30% rate
    Senior Mgmt
    functional heads
    20% rate
    Executive / CXO
    C-suite, direct reports
    0% (ineligible)
    Sector-wide aggregates derived from the Role Library
    Automobile — 66 roles across 28 nodes
    Eligibility (non-Exec roles): 88%
    Avg automation rate (incl. 0% Execs): 26.8%
    Avg automation rate (eligible only): 26.8%
    FMCG — 110 roles across 29 nodes
    Eligibility (non-Exec roles): 84.5%
    Avg automation rate (incl. 0% Execs): 25.0%
    Avg automation rate (eligible only): 25.0%

    The two sectors differ because FMCG carries a heavier Middle-Mgmt + Sales weighting (higher automation potential) offset by a larger Executive headcount share. Aggregates are derived directly from the 372-role Role Library, not hardcoded. Each node carries its own role mix and thus its own node-level eligibility + automation rate — see the Market Size Derivation card on each node.

    Q7Step 7 — AI price discountWhy a 50% AI price discount on displaced labour cost? ▼
    Definition

    Enterprise AI is priced BELOW the loaded cost of the labour it displaces. The 50% convention is the midpoint of the observed 40–60% pricing spread for enterprise AI copilots relative to per-seat loaded labour cost.

    Why 50%, not 100%
    • Adoption economics: if AI costs MORE than the labour it replaces, adoption is limited to the highest-value roles only. At 50% of labour cost, adoption broadens to mid-complexity mid-market roles where the ROI case is straightforward.
    • Observed pricing: Microsoft 365 Copilot ($30/user/month), GitHub Copilot Business ($19/user/month), Salesforce Einstein Copilot, Google Gemini for Workspace — loaded against their displaced-labour equivalent — land in the 40–60% range. The 50% midpoint is a conservative sizing anchor.
    • Avoids over-sizing: using 100% of labour cost would implicitly assume AI captures the full labour budget — which ignores the vendor margin the AI provider keeps and the operating cost of the AI itself.
    • India context: Indian enterprise AI pricing tends to sit at the lower end (40–50%) due to purchasing power and alternative labour availability, validating the 50% anchor as reasonable for this thesis.
    Sources

    a16z enterprise-AI pricing benchmarks · McKinsey State of AI 2024 · Gartner AI pricing research

    Sensitivity note: a shift from 50% → 40% moves total TAM from $1.8B to ~$1.8B; a shift to 60% moves it to ~$2.2B. All sector and node values scale linearly.

    Q8Ecosystem SAM sizingHow is the Ecosystem SAM sized? ($659M = $381M Auto + $278M FMCG) ▼

    The Ecosystem layer captures adjacent populations outside brand-owner payroll who do the same knowledge work and are addressable by the same AI products — dealer-network staff, ancillary supplier white-collar, distributor field sales, and merchandising channels. They are not on the brand-owner’s payroll but they sit inside the same AI-addressable workflow chain.

    PopulationHeadcountTAM ($M)Source
    Auto: FADA dealer-network staff~250K$130MFederation of Automobile Dealers Associations (FADA)
    Auto: ancillary supplier white-collar~200K$200MACMA FY24 Industry Presentation
    FMCG: Distributor + merchandiser field reps~500K$278MConfederation of Indian Industry (CII) + FICCI channel compensation surveys
    Ecosystem total~950K$659MAllocated per node via role-mix share

    Per-capita addressable dollars are lower than brand-owner KW because ecosystem roles skew toward sales-execution and administration (lower-pay cohorts). But the combined headcount is ~4× the brand-owner on-payroll base, making ecosystem the largest of the three TAM layers.

    Q9Cross-step reconciliationHow do Core SAM + Extended SAM + Ecosystem SAM reconcile per node? ▼

    Every node card displays up to two reconciled bands: (1) Brand-owner on-payroll SAM = Core SAM + Extended SAM combined for this node, computed from the 7-step chain (Universe × KW × Selected-nodes share × Salary × Role eligibility × Task AI rate × 50% discount); (2) Ecosystem SAM = share of the $659M channel-workforce layer allocated to this node by role-mix weighting. Nodes without channel relevance show only the brand-owner band. Per-node values sum EXACTLY to sector SAM totals by construction (no scaling factors).

    Worked example — FMCG Field Sales node:

    • Core + Extended $31M = 12,261 brand-owner field-sales KW × $14,469 × 0.88 × 0.38 × 0.50 (sector-avg factors; per-node weighting produces exact anchor). Sums with other FMCG selected nodes to $317M brand-owner SAM.
    • Ecosystem share — the $278M FMCG channel SAM (AICPDF distributors + FICCI merchandiser agencies) is a sector-level pool, not split per-node in the card display.

    Integrity check: Core SAM $1,065M + Extended SAM $105M + Ecosystem SAM $659M = $1.8B TAM. Brand-owner on-payroll (Core + Extended) across 4 sector-cells sums to $1,171M ($854M Auto + $317M FMCG). Per-node brand-owner SAM values sum EXACTLY to these sector totals by construction.

    Part 2 — Sector Deep-Dives
    28 selected workflow nodes across Automobile and FMCG — click any node to explore the problem, AI solution, and market size
    05 — Automobile Sector

    11 selected Auto workflow nodes — Vehicle Program Leadership, APQP/PPAP, Testing & Validation, Supply Chain, Operations Leadership, Dealership, After-Sales, Warranty, Finance, HR — totalling $1,235M sector TAM split across Core ($807M brand-owner) + Extended ($47M mid-market) + Ecosystem ($381M dealer + ancillary channel).

    Automobile — 11 nodes · $1,235M total addressable (Core + Extended + Ecosystem reconciled per card)
    The complete automobile value chain — from vehicle program inception through press-weld-paint-assembly to dealer sales and after-sales service. Each node identifies a specific knowledge work problem, the AI solution, existing products, and the enterprise market size.
    11 workflow nodes
    ~1,100 brand-owner firms
    ~587K addressable Knowledge Workers (148K Auto Core brand-owner selected-nodes + 450K ecosystem)
    $1,235M total addressable TAM
    How to read the value chain

    Cards flow left-to-right across 4 value chain pillars (Supply & Procurement · Manufacturing & Operations · Demand / Sales / Distribution · Support Functions). Click any selected card to see the problem, AI solution, existing products, and the node’s full 7-step SAM derivation. Each card shows:

    $XXM — node SAM (share of $1,235M Auto sector total) = node KW × $30,679 salary × node eligibility (72–98%) × node automation rate (27–46%) × 50% discount; per-node sums equal sector SAM by construction.
    X/10 — AI Readiness Score = average of AI Tech Maturity (vendors shipping at scale) + Knowledge Work Density (desk vs floor share) + India Deployment Evidence (Indian brand-owner case studies).
    TOP QUARTILE — score ≥ 8/10, highest-priority opportunities.

    Greyed-out cards are outside the 11-node selected scope — either physical/plant-OT workflows (vision QC, predictive maintenance), specialist engineering with capex-routed buying motion, or sub-scale KW headcount. Hover any greyed card for its specific exclusion reason.

    06 — FMCG Sector

    17 selected FMCG workflow nodes — S&OP, Procurement, Process Engineering, Quality, Marketing, Sales, Field Sales, Retail Execution, Customer Service, Finance, HR, Compliance, RGM — totalling $595M sector TAM split across Core ($259M brand-owner) + Extended ($58M mid-market) + Ecosystem ($278M distributor + merchandiser channel).

    FMCG — 17 nodes · $595M total addressable (Core + Extended + Ecosystem reconciled per card)
    The complete FMCG value chain — from R&D formulation and demand planning through continuous processing, packaging, and multi-tier distribution. BRSR-verified 48.6% Knowledge Worker share of permanent headcount at brand-owners, with highest Knowledge Worker/revenue density of any manufacturing sector.
    17 workflow nodes
    ~700 brand-owner firms
    ~605K addressable Knowledge Workers (105K FMCG Core brand-owner selected-nodes + 500K ecosystem)
    $595M total addressable TAM
    How to read the value chain

    Cards flow left-to-right across 4 value chain pillars (Supply & Procurement · Manufacturing & Operations · Demand / Sales / Distribution · Support Functions). Click any selected card to see the problem, AI solution, existing products, and the node’s full 7-step SAM derivation. Each card shows:

    $XXM — node SAM (share of $595M FMCG sector total) = node KW × $14,469 salary × node eligibility (75–98%) × node automation rate (28–45%) × 50% discount; per-node sums equal sector SAM by construction.
    X/10 — AI Readiness Score = average of AI Tech Maturity (vendors shipping at scale) + Knowledge Work Density (desk vs floor share) + India Deployment Evidence (Indian brand-owner case studies).
    TOP QUARTILE — score ≥ 8/10, highest-priority opportunities.

    Greyed-out cards are outside the 17-node selected scope — either physical/plant-OT workflows, specialist R&D, or sub-scale KW headcount. Hover any greyed card for its specific exclusion reason.

    Part 3 — Cross-Sector Priority Map
    28 workflow nodes across both Automobile and FMCG, ranked by enterprise TAM and AI readiness
    Summary — Across Both Sectors

    Cross-sector view of the 28 selected workflow nodes combined. The top 15 by enterprise TAM concentrate most of the $1.8B; the priority map plots them on TAM size × AI readiness; the value-chain segment view shows where Knowledge Worker headcount — and therefore TAM — actually sits.

    Where the $1.8B opportunity concentrates
    A cross-sector view of the 28 selected workflow nodes combined. The top 15 nodes by enterprise TAM concentrate most of the addressable market, and the Opportunity Priority Map shows which combinations of market size and AI readiness define the priority targets. The value chain segment breakdown shows where Knowledge Worker headcount — and therefore TAM — is actually concentrated across both Auto and FMCG.

    Opportunity Priority Map: Knowledge Worker Automation TAM vs. AI Readiness

    Each dot is one of 28 selected deep-dive nodes (11 Auto + 17 FMCG). X-axis = Knowledge Worker automation enterprise TAM ($M). Y-axis = AI Readiness score (1–10). Top-right quadrant = large markets with high AI readiness — the priority targets. Dot size scales with TAM. Hover any dot for details.

    Automobile
    FMCG
    Top quartile (score 8+)     Dot size = enterprise TAM

    TAM by Value Chain Segment

    Distribution of the $1,171M brand-owner on-payroll SAM (Core + Extended) across the four value chain pillars (combined Auto + FMCG across 28 selected nodes). Supply & Procurement is the largest slice because vehicle-program engineering + BRSR-anchored finance/HR nodes in Auto concentrate payroll there; Demand covers sales force, trade marketing, distributor channels. Ecosystem SAM ($659M) is not split here — see sector cards above.

    $449M
    38%
    Supply & Procurement
    $91M
    8%
    Manufacturing & Ops
    $404M
    35%
