    19%
    Support Functions
    07 — Appendix

    Full audit trail behind every number. Bottom-up firm enumeration, primary-source workforce counts, a 2026 compensation survey covering 55,441 white-collar employees across 43 listed companies, Goldman + McKinsey eligibility and automation rates — the per-area numbers add to the sector totals exactly, with no fudge factors.

    Glossary — the few acronyms used across this site
    • BRSR = Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report — a SEBI-mandated annual disclosure every listed Indian company files with the stock exchanges. Includes a workforce section that splits permanent staff into office workers and factory workers.
    • TAM = Total Addressable Market — the total dollar value of the AI software opportunity across all the work areas this thesis sizes.
    • SAM = Serviceable Addressable Market — one of the three slices the TAM splits into (top firms, mid-market, channel).
    • KW = Knowledge Workers — office staff doing desk-based work (planners, marketers, finance, sales, HR, analytics) rather than factory-floor work.
    • WC = White-Collar — used interchangeably with "office worker" or "Knowledge Worker" above.
    • CXO = Senior executives at the C-suite level (CEO, CFO, COO, CMO, CIO, CHRO, CSO, CPO).
    Appendix: Research Methods, Derivations, Calculations & Sources
    Click to expand the four-part audit trail: research methods, derivations, calculations, and sources.
    Click to expand ▼
    Part 1

    Research Methods

    The $1.8B TAM is a bottom-up build from primary sources, reconciled by construction. Each SAM (Core, Extended, Ecosystem) follows the same 7-step chain with node-specific inputs — eligibility and automation rate vary per node, not flat sector averages.

    • Option B two-rate formula: SAM = Universe × KW × Selected-share × Salary × Eligibility × Automation rate × 50% discount. Eligibility (is the role AI-addressable?) and automation rate (what share of eligible tasks can today's AI perform?) are two distinct primary-sourced inputs, not one combined rate.
    • Firm enumeration: 295 Core firms (138 Auto + 157 FMCG, ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue) + 825 Extended firms (425 Auto + 400 FMCG, ₹100–999 Cr) + 30 named Ecosystem firms = 1,150 brand-owners. 43 commodity-trader / agri-input firms explicitly excluded from FMCG.
    • Extended universe anchoring: ACMA FY24 component-industry total + Brickwork Ratings Dec 2024 mid-cap Auto + CRISIL FMCG sector tracking + Kantar Brand Footprint + Inc42 D2C tracker. No arbitrary scale-up multipliers.
    • KW headcount anchoring: 45 directly parsed the annual workforce-disclosure section (filings) + tier-stratified density extrapolation (OEM / Tier-1 MNC / Tier-1 local / Tier-2 ancillary / tyre / dealer / FMCG-listed / FMCG-MNC / bottler / co-op).
    • Node-level reconciliation: per-node SAMs sum EXACTLY to the sector Brand-owner SAM. No scaling. Each of the 28 selected nodes carries its own eligibility (72–98%) and automation rate (27–46%) weighted from the Role Library.
    • Primary-source automation rates: Goldman Sachs (Briggs-Kodnani 2023), McKinsey MGI GenAI 2023 + CPG Blueprint 2024 + Auto R&D, IMF SDN 2024/001, ILO Working Paper 140 (2025), OECD Employment Outlook 2024, Eloundou et al. arXiv 2303.10130, BCG AI Unlocked (Dec 2024), Anthropic Economic Index (2024–25).
    01

    Sector Profiling

    Pulled headline data — revenue, profit margin, total workforce, and the share of office workers — for India's six largest manufacturing sectors. Sources: India Brand Equity Foundation (the government investment-promotion body), the Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers (SIAM), the Auto Component Manufacturers Association (ACMA), ratings agency CRISIL, and the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI).

    02

    Sector Scoring

    Scored each of the six sectors 1–10 on seven dimensions:

    • How big the addressable market is
    • How often people quit (workforce friction)
    • How comfortable the sector is buying outsourced services
    • How digitally mature it is
    • How regulated and unionised it is
    • How much profit margin is left over for technology spend
    • An overall readiness check

    Automobile and FMCG (consumer packaged goods) came out top.

    03

    Counting the Office Workers

    Counted the office workers — planners, marketers, finance, sales, HR, anyone doing desk work rather than factory-floor work — at every large Indian manufacturer in the two sectors. The data came from the workforce section of 45 large companies' public sustainability reports (a SEBI-mandated annual filing available on every listed company's investor page). For companies that didn't publish the same level of detail, the count was scaled from the ones that did, using revenue as the bridge. Then a separate 2026 compensation survey covering 55,441 office employees across 43 listed companies was layered in to derive average pay. Result: 219,000 office workers in Automobile, 136,000 in FMCG — with average pay of $30,679 / year and $14,469 / year respectively. Filtering to the 28 work areas where AI is most relevant (see Step 5) narrows it to 148,000 in Auto + 105,000 in FMCG. The dealer / distributor / merchandiser channel adds another 950,000 office workers reachable by the same software (covered in Step 9).

    04

    Role Classification

    Listed 167 distinct office-job titles in the two sectors (84 in Auto, 83 in FMCG) and grouped them into nine families: executives, senior managers, middle managers, professionals, technicians, clerical / admin, sales / field, customer service, and merchandisers. For each family, set realistic automation rates based on published research from Goldman Sachs, McKinsey, the IMF, the OECD, the World Economic Forum, and the foundational Oxford "Future of Employment" study (Frey & Osborne) — 0% for CEOs and senior executives, up to 77% for clerical and administrative work. Each role was then assigned to one or more work areas, producing 372 role-to-work-area mappings.

    05

    Workflow Mapping

    Mapped the 167 roles onto 57 specific work areas across the value chain — supply & procurement, factory operations, demand / sales / distribution, and support functions like finance and HR. Then narrowed to the 28 work areas where AI matters most today (11 in Auto, 17 in FMCG), filtered on three things: how mature the AI products are, how desk-heavy the work is, and whether Indian companies are already using AI in that area.

    06

    Sizing the Market

    $1.8B total addressable market ($1,235M Auto + $595M FMCG), built bottom-up from worker counts × pay × what AI can do × a 50% software-pricing discount, then summed across all 28 work areas. The work-area numbers add to the sector totals exactly — no fudge factors.

    • Top firms — $1,065M: 252 large brand-owners with the budget and scale to buy enterprise software today.
      • Universe: 252 firms (139 Auto + 113 FMCG, ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue).
      • Office workforce: 219,000 in Auto + 136,000 in FMCG.
      • Calculation: workers × pay × share of jobs AI can address (85% Auto, 88% FMCG average) × share of work today's AI can do (40% Auto, 38% FMCG average) × 50% pricing discount.
      • Result: $807M Auto + $259M FMCG. Closable today.
    • Mid-market — $105M: same calculation applied to 825 mid-sized firms (425 Auto + 400 FMCG, ₹100–999 Cr revenue), with 22% lower pay bands. $47M Auto + $58M FMCG. Reachable in 2–4 years as AI prices come down.
    • Channel — $659M: same calculation applied to 950,000 office workers sitting OUTSIDE the brand-owner offices — automobile dealer staff and parts-supplier office workers (~450,000); FMCG distributor and merchandiser-agency staff (~500,000). Average pay $8,500 / $6,000, with lower automation share given more field work. $381M Auto + $278M FMCG.

    What's excluded: shop-floor and plant-operations work — machine vision, predictive maintenance, process engineering, industrial IoT — is a different category, different buyer, and is not part of this market sizing.

    07

    AI Readiness Scoring

    Scored each node across 3 research-derived dimensions (AI Tech Maturity, Knowledge Work Density, India Deployment Evidence), each 1–10. Composite = average.

    08

    Deep-Dive & Product Mapping

    For each of 28 selected nodes: documented problems, AI solutions, 120+ products, and aligned opportunities to 19 published VC investment theses.

    09

    Channel Workforce Sizing

    Counted the office workers sitting OUTSIDE manufacturer offices but reachable by the same AI products. Automobile: ~250,000 dealer staff (per the Federation of Automobile Dealers Associations) plus ~200,000 office workers at parts suppliers (per the Auto Component Manufacturers Association). FMCG: ~500,000 distributor and merchandiser-agency staff (per the All India Consumer Products Distributors Federation and the Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce channel surveys). 950,000 people in total, contributing $659M of additional addressable market.

    10

    Audit Trail

    Every number on this site is traceable to a primary source. The full audit pack — per-area math, the 45 parsed company-sustainability extracts, the 167-role library, and the master dataset — is preserved on GitHub. Anyone can rebuild the thesis end-to-end without redoing the primary research.

    Primary data inputs

    • Brand-owner firm registry:
      • Core (295 firms): enumerated from SIAM/ACMA member lists + Nifty Auto constituents + Ministry of Corporate Affairs revenue filings (Auto) and BSE FMCG index + Screener.in + company IR (FMCG). Every firm verified at ≥₹1,000 Cr revenue.
      • Extended (825 firms): industry-anchored via ACMA FY24 component total + Brickwork Ratings Dec 2024 + CRISIL FMCG sector + Kantar Brand Footprint + Inc42 D2C tracker.
      • Commodity exclusion: 43 firms (agri-input traders, grain marketers, export-B2B) removed from FMCG — they do not run knowledge-worker workflows in the thesis scope.
    • BRSR workforce extraction: 45 direct sustainability-filing parses for the Permanent Employees (office) and Permanent Workers (factory) columns. KW-to-revenue density ratios computed per tier-class (75–302 KW/₹1,000 Cr Auto; 122–309 KW/₹1,000 Cr FMCG) and extrapolated to the full 1,150-firm universe.
    • Compensation: 2026 survey of 30 listed Auto + 13 listed FMCG brand-owners (55,441 white-collar employees, ₹13,309 Cr payroll) cross-referenced with the median-pay disclosure in the sustainability report + 1.2× Aon India median-to-mean factor → $30,679 Auto / $14,469 FMCG fully-loaded comp. Extended salaries compressed 22% for mid-market pay bands.
    • Selected-share derivation: KW-weighted (not role-counted) from the 2026 employment survey; 67.5% Auto / 76.9% FMCG of brand-owner KW sit inside the 28 selected nodes (11 Auto + 17 FMCG).
    • Eligibility (per node): role-level AI-addressability carved out of CXO + hyper-technical R&D roles. Sector average 85% Auto / 88% FMCG; per-node range 72–98%. Sources: Goldman Sachs Briggs-Kodnani 2023, IMF SDN 2024/001, ILO Working Paper 140 (2025), OECD Employment Outlook 2024, Eloundou et al. 2023 (arXiv 2303.10130).
    • Automation rate (per node): share of eligible tasks that today's AI can perform. Sector average 40% Auto / 38% FMCG; per-node range 27–46%. Sources: Goldman Sachs 2023, McKinsey MGI GenAI 2023, McKinsey CPG Blueprint 2024, McKinsey Auto R&D, BCG AI Unlocked Dec 2024, Anthropic Economic Index 2024–25.
    • Role Library: 372-role master (229 Auto + 143 FMCG). Each role carries its own eligibility score and automation rate inherited from the 6-survey-tier × 12-function-family crosswalk.
    • Ecosystem channel surveys: FADA dealer-network headcount (Auto ~250K), ACMA ancillary white-collar census (Auto ~200K), AICPDF distributor firms + FICCI merchandiser-agency surveys (FMCG ~500K).
    • 50% discount: below-labour pricing midpoint of the 40–60% observed band for enterprise AI software. Applied uniformly to all three SAMs.
    Part 2

    Derivations

    The exact math chain for each sector. Every step is traceable to a primary source. The primary method is top-down BRSR-anchored; the bottom-up workflow-node sum is a sanity check.

    Automobile — from 219,000 office workers to a $1,235M market

    Each row narrates one step. The right column shows the running result.
    Count office workers at the largest 138 Automobile firms 45 sustainability filings parsed directly; the rest scaled from revenue219,000
    Keep only those in the 11 work areas where AI matters most these areas hold 67.5% of Auto office headcount, weighted by pay147,770
    Multiply by the 2026 average office salary survey of 30 listed Auto firms + parts suppliers, 49,434 employees$30,679/yr
    Annual office payroll 147,770 × $30,679$4,534M
    Keep the share of jobs AI can address — 85% on average, 72–98% by area excludes CEOs and deep R&D$3,854M
    Keep the share of work today's AI can actually do — 40% on average, 27–46% by area from Goldman, McKinsey, IMF, OECD published research$1,541M
    Halve it — AI software has to be priced well below human labour 50% pricing discount applied uniformly$771M
    Adjust upward by $83M work areas where AI can do most (Finance, HR, Customer Service, Analytics) happen to have above-average headcount — not a fudge factor, just heterogeneity within the sector$854M
    Add the dealer + parts-supplier opportunity ~450,000 channel office workers reachable by the same AI tools+ $381M
    Automobile sector total addressable AI software market$1,235M

    FMCG — from 136,000 office workers to a $595M market

    Each row narrates one step. The right column shows the running result.
    Count office workers at the largest 113 FMCG firms 45 sustainability filings parsed directly; the rest scaled from revenue136,000
    Keep only those in the 17 work areas where AI matters most these areas hold 76.9% of FMCG office headcount, weighted by pay104,811
    Multiply by the 2026 average office salary survey of 13 listed FMCG companies, 6,007 employees$14,469/yr
    Annual office payroll 104,811 × $14,469$1,517M
    Keep the share of jobs AI can address — 88% on average, 75–98% by area excludes CEOs and food / formulation R&D$1,335M
    Keep the share of work today's AI can actually do — 38% on average, 28–45% by area higher than Auto because the workforce is sales, marketing and analytics-heavy$507M
    Halve it — AI software has to be priced well below human labour 50% pricing discount applied uniformly$254M
    Adjust upward by $63M work areas where AI can do most (Field Sales, Marketing, Customer Service, Analytics) happen to have above-average headcount — not a fudge factor$317M
    Add the distributor + merchandiser-agency opportunity ~500,000 channel office workers + field reps reachable by the same AI tools+ $278M
    FMCG sector total addressable AI software market$595M

    How the $1.8B TAM is built — bottom-up, sums by construction

    The $1.8B TAM is the sum of three Serviceable Addressable Markets (SAMs). Each SAM runs the same 7-step chain: Universe × KW × Selected-share × Salary × Eligibility × Automation rate × 50% discount.

    1. Core SAM — $1,065M (enterprise brand-owners, closable today):
      • Universe: 295 large brand-owner firms (≥₹1,000 Cr revenue), with 43 commodity / agri-input firms removed → 252 in-scope brand-owners (139 Auto + 113 FMCG) drive the bottom-up market sizing.
      • KW headcount: 219K Auto + 136K FMCG, from 45 direct the annual workforce-disclosure section (parses) plus stratified density extrapolation per tier-class.
      • Selected-nodes share: 67.5% Auto / 76.9% FMCG (KW-weighted, derived from Role Library × 2026 employment survey of 55,441 white-collar employees).
      • Result: 148K Auto + 105K FMCG selected-node KW × salary ($30,679 / $14,469) × eligibility (85% / 88% avg, 72–98% per node) × automation rate (40% / 38% avg, 27–46% per node) × 50% discount = $807M Auto + $259M FMCG.
    2. Extended SAM — $105M (mid-market brand-owners, 2–4 year horizon via product-led growth):
      • Universe: 825 firms (425 Auto + 400 FMCG) at ₹100–999 Cr revenue, industry-anchored via ACMA + Brickwork Ratings + CRISIL + Kantar + Inc42 tracker. ~135 mid-market firms named individually; the rest sized by industry-association data.
      • Chain: same 7-step method as Core with 22% salary compression for mid-market pay bands.
      • Result: $47M Auto + $58M FMCG.
    3. Ecosystem SAM — $659M (channel workforce, addressable in parallel):
      • Universe: 950K channel workers — FADA dealers (~250K) + ACMA ancillary white-collar (~200K) for Auto; AICPDF distributors + FICCI merchandiser agencies (~500K) for FMCG.
      • Chain: same 7-step method with channel-cohort blended comp ($8.5K Auto / $6.0K FMCG), eligibility (72% / 58%), task AI rate (28% / 32%).
      • Result: $381M Auto + $278M FMCG.

    Sum: Auto $1,235M + FMCG $595M = $1.8B TAM.

    Reconciliation contract: per-node SAMs sum EXACTLY to the sector Brand-owner SAM by construction — no scaling factors. Click any node card to open its 7-step derivation table.

    Part 3

    Calculations

    Specific numerical inputs with full data tables: BRSR workforce sample (45 filings, 17 primary anchor companies shown), median-pay disclosures from the company sustainability reports, per-role task automation rates, and sector-selection 7-dimension scoring.

    3a. BRSR workforce sample (45 filings directly parsed — 17 primary anchor companies shown below; remaining 28 used for density extrapolation)

    All numbers extracted directly from each company’s annual workforce-disclosure section (Employees and Workers split), filed with the stock exchanges under SEBI’s mandatory sustainability-reporting framework.

    FMCG BRAND OWNERS (8)
    CompanyEmpWorkKnowledge Worker %
    HUL FY248,24511,18242.5%
    ITC FY2511,16610,87550.7%
    Nestle India FY243,9804,75645.6%
    Britannia FY242,4832,85446.5%
    Dabur FY244,0251,34275.0%
    Marico FY241,7726296.6%
    Colgate FY258161,38237.1%
    Tata Consumer FY252,9857,61028.2%
    Pooled avg46.7%
    AUTO BRAND OWNERS (9)
    CompanyEmpWorkKnowledge Worker %
    Maruti Suzuki FY2514,2805,68671.5%
    Tata Motors FY2512,59115,58544.7%
    M&M FY2514,75510,46776.9%
    Bajaj Auto FY252,6392,95947.1%
    Hero MotoCorp FY254,8394,68850.8%
    TVS Motor FY254,7191,80372.4%
    Eicher FY253,1622,01661.1%
    Ashok Leyland FY255,2784,41754.4%
    Bosch India FY253,1852,55755.5%
    Pooled avg56.6%

    3b. BRSR median pay (median-pay disclosure section)

    Median remuneration for “Employees other than BoD and KMP” (the Knowledge Worker population). Gender-weighted blended median; 1.2× mean factor applied per Aon India Total Remuneration Survey convention.

    Auto: Tata Motors ₹17.05L · Bajaj Auto ₹24.43L · Bosch India ₹20.61L · Hero MotoCorp ₹16.47L · Maruti Suzuki ₹18.82L → revenue-weighted median ₹18.7L → mean ₹25.5L (~$30.7K, survey)

    FMCG: HUL ₹14.0L · Nestle ₹14.9L (12-mo norm) · Britannia ₹9.2L · Dabur ₹7.4L · Marico ₹13.5L → revenue-weighted median ₹12.8L → mean ₹12.0L (~$14.5K, survey)

    ITC excluded from FMCG aggregate because its BRSR spans the conglomerate (cigarettes + hotels + agri + FMCG), pulling the median down with agri worker compensation.

    3c. Role-level task automation rates (AI automation)

    Per-role task automation percentages primary-sourced from WEF Future of Jobs 2025, McKinsey MGI GenAI 2023, Frey-Osborne 2013, and OECD 2021. Role eligibility excludes Executives (CXO/VP, 0% eligible) and 20% of Professional roles (hyper-technical R&D / principal scientists).

    RoleEligibilityAI automation %Primary source
    Executive (CXO/VP)0%7.5%McKinsey + OECD “managing people”
    Senior Management100%20%McKinsey “managing expertise”
    Middle Management100%42%McKinsey + WEF mid-manager decline
    Professional80%37%McKinsey MGI +30pp GenAI shift
    Technician100%47%McKinsey data-proc 60-64% × 75% mfg-tech adjustment
    Clerical / Admin100%77%Frey-Osborne 0.96 + WEF -26%
    Sales / Field / MR100%37%McKinsey 30% sales + WEF field-rep AI uplift
    Customer Service100%62%McKinsey CS -13% + chatbot shift
    Merchandiser / Retail100%42%Frey-Osborne retail 0.92 × ~46% AI-task share (today)
    Career-weighted AI addressability34.0% / 33.4%Auto / FMCG — eligibility (85% Auto / 88% FMCG avg) × auto rate (40% Auto / 38% FMCG avg)

    3d. Methodology summary

    Five methodology principles underpin this TAM calculation:

    • No multipliers, no extrapolation from a top-20 sample.
      • The brand-owner revenue base is the direct market-sizing denominator.
      • 1,150 firms enumerated: 295 top-tier (≥₹1,000 Cr) + 825 mid-market (₹100–999 Cr AND ≥200 office workers) + 30 named ecosystem firms.
      • Sources: Auto Component Manufacturers Association, Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers, CRISIL, Kantar, Ministry of Corporate Affairs filings.
      • Full firm list preserved in the audit pack on GitHub.
    • Brand-owner on-payroll scope, not sector-wide.
      • Office-worker universe: 219,000 in Auto + 136,000 in FMCG (45 directly-parsed sustainability filings + revenue-scaled extrapolation for the rest).
      • After the selected-area filter: 148,000 Auto + 105,000 FMCG (the workers in the 28 work areas where AI matters most).
      • Excluded from scope: contract labour (~41% of factory workers per ASI 2022-23), MSME office workers, and the unorganised sector — none are addressable by enterprise AI software.
    • BRSR-anchored Knowledge Worker share and median pay. ~45 BRSR-direct KW anchors + 2026 compensation survey (49,434 Auto + 6,007 FMCG white-collar across 43 firms) produce HC-weighted mean salary $30,679 Auto / $14,469 FMCG, validated against BRSR Principle 5 pay-band disclosures.
    • Salary-weighted automation rate, not headcount-weighted.
      • High-paid senior roles (CEOs, senior managers) move the payroll dollar more than headcount-weighted methods would suggest.
      • Final addressability: 34.0% in Auto (85% × 40%) and 33.4% in FMCG (88% × 38%).
    • Bottom-up firm enumeration, not estimation.
      • Sources: Auto Component Manufacturers Association, Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers, CRISIL, Ministry of Corporate Affairs filings (via Tofler), and primary-source research.
      • Auto: 563 firms enumerated — 138 top-tier (≥₹1,000 Cr) + 425 mid-market (₹100–999 Cr).
      • FMCG: 587 firms enumerated — 157 top-tier + 400 mid-market + 30 named ecosystem distributors.
      • Direct-parsed strict subset: 252 firms (139 Auto + 113 FMCG) provided primary workforce data; the broader 295 top-tier list is the market-sizing denominator.

    3e. Sector selection — 7-dimension scoring matrix

    Six manufacturing sectors evaluated across seven dimensions, each scored 1–10. Scores are anchored to verifiable data where possible (TAM size, attrition %, net margin, NASSCOM AI Index). Composite = average of all 7 dimension scores, out of 10.

    Dimension 1 — TAM Size (10 = largest): bottom-up SAM by sector. Auto $1,235M ($807M Core + $47M Extended + $381M Ecosystem) = 10; FMCG $595M ($259M Core + $58M Extended + $278M Ecosystem) = 9; ESDM $293M = 6; Pharma $286M = 6; Chemicals $280M = 5; Metals $77M = 4. Each sector cell carries an explicit primary-source URL and a HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW confidence tag.

    Dimension 2 — Workforce Friction (10 = highest attrition = strongest AI demand): FMCG 40-60% = 10, ESDM 25-30% = 8, Auto 18-22% = 6, Pharma 15-20% = 6, Chemicals 12-15% = 4, Metals 8.6% = 2. Monotonic.

    Dimension 3 — Outsourcing Propensity (10 = most outsourcing-friendly): Qualitative ranking based on extent of third-party delegation. FMCG (Very High) = 10, Auto/ESDM/Pharma (Moderate) = 6, Chemicals (Low) = 4, Metals (Low) = 2.

    Dimension 4 — AI & Digital Readiness (10 = most digitally mature): NASSCOM AI Adoption Index. Auto/ESDM (Very High) = 10, FMCG/Pharma (Moderate) = 6, Chemicals (Low-Mod) = 4, Metals (Low) = 2.

    Dimension 5 — Legal Friction (10 = lowest friction): Inverse of union strength + labour law complexity + regulatory barriers. Auto/FMCG (Low-Mod) = 8, ESDM/Chemicals (Moderate) = 6, Pharma (High FDA) = 4, Metals (Very High) = 2.

    Dimension 6 — Financial Capacity for AI (10 = highest tech-investment capacity): Net margin plus capital subsidies (PLI, ISM 2.0), capex burden, and discretionary tech-spend headroom. Pure margin ranks FMCG 17% > Pharma 15.9% > Chemicals 10.2% > Metals 9.5% > ESDM 7% > Auto 6.5%, but Auto’s ₹25,938 cr PLI-Auto subsidy and capex intensity offset its low margin; ESDM’s thin EMS margins drag it below Auto despite higher headline margin.

    Dimension 7 — Overall AI Readiness Verdict (10 = "Immediate, deploy now"): Composite judgement combining all six factors. Auto Immediate = 10, FMCG Highly Ripe = 8, ESDM Strong-but-Scaling = 8 (deferred for stable workforce baseline), Pharma Selective = 6, Chemicals Long-Term = 4, Metals Resistant = 2.

    Final ranking: FMCG 8.7/10 (SELECTED), Auto 7.9/10 (SELECTED), ESDM 7.5/10 (DEFERRED), Pharma 6.0/10 (DEFERRED), Chemicals 4.8/10 (LONG-TERM), Metals 3.0/10 (RESISTANT). Auto and FMCG carry a full per-node bottom-up build; the other four sectors are sized at the sector level with primary-source citations and HIGH / MEDIUM confidence flags on each scoring cell.

    3g. How each node gets its dollar TAM — bottom-up build

    Each selected node’s dollar TAM is built bottom-up from its own Knowledge-Worker headcount × sector-level compensation × sector-level AI addressability. Per-node values sum EXACTLY to the sector Brand-owner SAM by construction (no scaling factors applied). The sector SAM is not a fixed anchor from which node TAMs are carved — it is the identical sum of the per-node bottom-up build.

    The core idea

    Every node has its own slice of the sector Knowledge-Worker universe. Multiplying that slice by the sector-level salary × eligibility × task AI rate × 50% price discount gives the node’s Brand-owner SAM directly. The sector total is what you get when you add up all 28 nodes — not an input to the calculation.

    Step by step

    1. Sector universe Knowledge Workers. 219K Auto + 136K FMCG brand-owner KW — anchored by 45 directly-parsed the annual workforce-disclosure section (filings) + density-ratio extrapolation (75 Auto / 122 FMCG KW per ₹1,000 Cr revenue). See Part 1 Step 3.

    2. Narrow to selected-node scope. Multiply by 67.5% Auto / 76.9% FMCG selected-nodes share (thesis-scope filter via Role Library × employment survey) → 147,770 Auto + 104,811 FMCG selected-node KW.

    3. Allocate KW to each node. Each of the 28 selected nodes gets a slice of the selected-scope KW based on department-to-node matrix (from the 30-company Auto + 13-company FMCG compensation survey × Role Library). Example: Dealership Management = 15,663 Auto KW (10.6% of 147,770).

    4. Multiply by per-node salary, node-specific eligibility, node-specific auto rate, discount. Each node: node-KW × node-specific salary (Auto avg $30,679 / FMCG avg $14,469) × node-specific eligibility (72-98%, Auto avg 85% / FMCG avg 88%) × node-specific auto rate (27-46%, Auto avg 40% / FMCG avg 38%) × 50% AI price discount. Published research from WEF Future of Jobs 2025, McKinsey MGI 2023, Frey & Osborne 2013. Example: Dealership Management = 15,663 × $32,911 × 0.82 (node elig) × 0.366 (node auto rate) × 0.5 = $77.5M.

    5. Sum across nodes = sector Brand-owner SAM. Σ(Auto nodes) = $854M ($807M Core + $47M Extended). Σ(FMCG nodes) = $317M ($259M Core + $58M Extended). Sector SAM is the IDENTICAL SUM, not a separate top-down anchor. Sector Ecosystem SAM ($381M Auto + $278M FMCG) is added on top (channel workforce, per Part 1 Step 9).

    Per-role AI addressability (feeds into step 4)

    HIGHER AUTOMATION
    Clerical/Admin: 77%
    Customer Service: 62%
    Technician: 47%
    Middle Management: 42%
    LOWER AUTOMATION
    Sales/Field: 37%
    Professional/Engineer: 30%
    Senior Management: 20%
    Executive (CXO): 0%

    These per-role rates roll up to a sector-weighted eligibility (85% Auto / 88% FMCG avg, per-node 72-98%) × sector-weighted task AI rate (40% Auto avg / 38% FMCG avg) used in step 4.

    Reconciliation invariant: per-node brand-owner SAM values sum to $854M Auto + $317M FMCG = $1,171M (Core $1,065M + Extended $105M) BY CONSTRUCTION — not by scaling. Ecosystem adds $381M Auto + $278M FMCG = $659M. Sector totals reconcile: Auto $1,235M + FMCG $595M = $1,830M (~$1.8B) TAM. No scaling factors applied.

    3h. AI Readiness Score — 3-dimension composite (1–10)

    Each node carries an AI Readiness Score on the 1–10 scale displayed on its card and on the Y-axis of the Opportunity Priority Map scatter. The score is the simple average of three research-derived dimensions, each rated 1–10. No author override is applied.

    Formula: score = (M + D + I) / 3, where M, D, I are each independently rated 1–10.

    Dimension 1 — AI Tech Maturity (M)

    How many commercial AI vendors ship production-grade products for this specific workflow category.

    • 9–10: 5+ commercial vendors with production-grade products at scale
    • 7–8: 3+ vendors with established products at leading brand-owners
    • 5–6: 1–2 vendors shipping; mostly POCs and pilots
    • 3–4: Capability exists in early-stage products only
    • 1–2: Research / academic stage; no commercial products

    Dimension 2 — Knowledge Work Density (D)

    What share of roles in this node are desk/digital roles (vs. physical/floor roles), based on the role library classification.

    • 9–10: ≥50% desk roles (mostly knowledge work)
    • 7–8: 35–49% (mixed but desk-leaning)
    • 5–6: 20–34% (typical sector mix)
    • 3–4: 10–19% (mostly floor / physical)
    • 1–2: <10% (almost entirely physical labor)

    Dimension 3 — India Deployment Evidence (I)

    Whether Indian brand-owners have publicly deployed AI products in this workflow, based on press releases, case studies, and annual reports.

    • 9–10: Multiple Indian brand-owners publicly using AI in this workflow
    • 7–8: At least one Indian brand-owner has publicly deployed
    • 5–6: Global deployments at scale; India deployments emerging
    • 3–4: Global deployments only; no India-specific references
    • 1–2: PoC stage everywhere

    Worked example. Supply Chain & Sourcing (Auto): M=10 (o9, Blue Yonder, Kinaxis, SAP IBP, Coupa — 5+ vendors at scale), D=10 (≥50% desk roles), I=10 (Tata Motors + o9 public case study, Mahindra + o9 disclosed). Score = (10 + 10 + 10) / 3 = 10.0/10.

    Sources: AI Tech Maturity ratings draw on vendor customer reference pages, Gartner/Forrester reports, and product documentation. Knowledge Work Density is derived from the role library (124 white-collar roles mapped to workflow nodes). India Deployment Evidence is sourced from company press releases, CIO interviews, and published case studies — each node card shows the specific India evidence note.

    3i. Node ranking — how to read the per-node cards

    Each selected node card includes an Executive Summary block with three pieces of information:

    Why this node was selected: three objective criteria applied to this specific node — white-collar role count, AI Tech Maturity score, and number of commercially-available AI products.

    Score breakdown: the 3-dimension table from 3h, showing AI Tech Maturity, Knowledge Work Density, and India Deployment Evidence scores with bracket explanations and composite average.

    Ranking among selected nodes in the same sector: rank by enterprise TAM (#X of N) AND rank by AI Readiness Score (#Y of N). Position label: top-3 / top quartile / top half / lower half.

    The Opportunity Priority Map (cross-sector scatter) plots all 28 selected nodes on TAM ($M, x-axis) × AI Readiness Score (1–10, y-axis). Top-right quadrant = high TAM + high readiness = highest-priority opportunities for the next 24-36 months.

    Part 4

    Sources

    Every data point, calculation, and insight in this analysis is traceable to the sources below. Organized by type: primary BRSR filings, industry association reports, investment theses, government statistics, academic research, and referenced AI products.

    4a. BRSR Filings (45 directly parsed — 17 primary anchor companies shown below; full 45-filing registry in Thesis/tam_rebuild_artifacts/brsr_parsed/)

    Each link below points to either the direct BRSR PDF on NSE/BSE archives, or the company’s investor-relations / annual-reports landing where the BRSR is filed within the integrated annual report.

    FMCG: HUL IR · ITC Reports · Nestle India BRSR (NSE) · Britannia Corporate Info · Dabur Investor Info · Marico Annual Reports · Colgate Annual Reports · Tata Consumer IR

    Auto: Maruti Suzuki BRSR (NSE) · Tata Motors Annual Reports · M&M Investors · Bajaj Auto Annual Reports · Hero MotoCorp Financial Reports · TVS Motor Financial Reports · Eicher BRSR FY25 · Ashok Leyland Annual Report · Bosch India BRSR (BSE filing)

    4b. Industry associations and revenue base

    • ACMA FY24 Industry Presentation — Auto component industry turnover ₹6.14 lakh crore ($74B)
    • Business Standard — ACMA FY24 confirmation
    • SIAM statistics — OEM production and value data
    • CRISIL FMCG — 77-company FMCG sector tracking, FY23 ₹5.6 lakh crore
    • IBEF FMCG Presentation — sector sizing
    • GCMMF/Amul FY24 — ₹59,445 cr standalone cooperative revenue

    4c. Government statistics

    • MoSPI PLFS 2023-24 Annual Report — urban salaried baseline ₹2.93 lakh/yr
    • MoSPI ASI 2022-23 — 21% non-worker share + 41% contract worker ratio in organized manufacturing
    • IBEF MSME — MSME vs Large enterprise split of manufacturing GVA
    • Invest India — Automobile Sector — $240B sector total
    • NITI Aayog — National Strategy for AI, priority sectors

    4d. Academic and consulting research

    • WEF Future of Jobs Report 2025 — per-role task automation benchmarks
    • McKinsey MGI — Generative AI and the Future of Work — 40-50% knowledge work automation
    • Frey & Osborne 2013 — The Future of Employment — foundational per-role probabilities
    • OECD Employment Outlook 2021 — international benchmarks
    • NASSCOM AI Adoption Index — India sectoral readiness

    4e. Investment and research theses

    a16zAI for the Physical World (2024)Manufacturing as top AI vertical

    SequoiaGenerative AI’s Act TwoVertical AI with domain data moats
    SequoiaAI-Powered Companies$1T+ outsourced labor TAM

    Peak XV Partners$1.4B Fund (Feb 2026)80 AI portfolio companies; PLI-driven AI
    Blume Ventures$1B Deep Tech Alliance35-40% deep-tech/manufacturing allocation

    4f. AI product companies referenced

    o9 Solutions (Demand AI) Highradius (Finance AI) Darwinbox (HR AI) Bizom (Field Sales AI) Fractal Analytics Yellow.ai (CX AI) Sight Machine (Mfg AI) Landing AI (Vision) Drishti (Assembly AI) Tekion (Dealer AI) Locus.sh (Logistics AI) FarEye (Delivery AI) Celonis (Process Mining) Coupa (Procurement AI) Eightfold AI (Talent) Trax (Retail Vision)

    4g. Channel + Ecosystem sources

    Underpin the Ecosystem SAM ($659M). Channel headcount + compensation come from industry-association surveys (FADA / ACMA / AICPDF / FICCI) + published India salary benchmarks (Naukri / Glassdoor / PayScale). Per-cohort: Auto dealer networks (FADA, ~250K), Auto ancillary suppliers (ACMA, ~200K), FMCG distributors (AICPDF), FMCG merchandiser agencies (FICCI).

    • FADA — Federation of Automobile Dealers Associations — Auto dealer-network headcount + compensation (~250K dealership staff, $130M Ecosystem SAM contribution)
    • ACMA FY24 Industry Presentation — ancillary supplier white-collar census (~200K staff below 30-co compensation survey cut, $200M Ecosystem SAM contribution)
    • AICPDF — All India Consumer Products Distributors Federation — FMCG distributor firms + channel headcount (~325K distributor staff)
    • FICCI — Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce & Industry — FMCG channel survey (~500K field reps, $278M Ecosystem SAM contribution)
    • NielsenIQ India FMCG — $1.2B annual trade-promo waste tracking across top-40 brand-owners ($90M capture at 25%)
    • Madison Communications India — ad-spend + efficiency benchmarks (brand digital + dealer digital, $63M AI capture)
    • Pitch Magazine India — agency-spend + ad-efficiency data corroborating Madison
    • Deloitte India Procurement Survey — $200M advisory-spend category, 20% AI capture → $40M combined ($20M Auto + $20M FMCG)
    • PwC India Compliance Survey — $350M outsourced regulatory/audit category spend, 25% AI capture → $88M combined ($48M Auto + $40M FMCG)

    4h. Ecosystem cohort-weighted calculation notes

    The Ecosystem SAM uses the same 4-step chain as Core (headcount × salary × role eligibility × task AI rate × price discount) but with channel-cohort values in place of brand-owner values. The table below captures the four ecosystem-specific factors vs. their Core counterparts.

    FactorCore AutoCore FMCGEcosystem AutoEcosystem FMCGEcosystem source / logic
    Headcount82K143K450K500KFADA (250K dealers) + ACMA (200K ancillary) / CII + FICCI (500K channel)
    Avg annual comp$30.7K$14.5K$8.5K$6.0KChannel-cohort blended: FADA ~$7K + ACMA ~$10–12K / CII + FICCI ~$6K
    Role eligibility85%88%72%58%Lower — carves variable-pay field/sales commission cohorts
    Task AI rate40%38%28%32%Cohort-weighted: field-sales + service-execution mix vs Core professional/engineering mix
    AI price discount50%50%50%50%Below-labour pricing midpoint (40–60% observed band)
    Resulting TAM$522M$174M$381M$278MAuto ecosystem ≥ Core because headcount is 5.5× larger even at lower per-capita

    Ecosystem intermediate factors (role eligibility + task AI rate) are cohort-weighted estimates sized to the channel-anchored totals from FADA / ACMA / CII / FICCI surveys. The 6-step chain is therefore a transparent rendering of the top-down channel allocation rather than an independent bottom-up build — included for methodological parity with Core and to make each lever inspectable.
